title: Equivariant Cohomology and the (Symplectic) Diffeotype of Complexity-One Four-Manifolds
speaker: Tara Holm
abstract: In this talk, we will explore the relationship between the geometry and topology of a complexity-one four-manifold and the combinatorial data that encode it. We will use a generators-and-relations description for the even part of the equivariant cohomology of the manifold to see what geometric aspects the equivariant cohomology determines. Namely, it allows us to reconstruct the diffeotype but not the complex structure. The talk will be driven by specific examples and pictures. It is based on joint work with Liat Kessler and Susan Tolman.

proof_stages.label: Guiding question via toric combinatorics
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames the problem of what geometry and topology of Hamiltonian circle actions are encoded by equivariant cohomology, using toric polytopes and fans to preview that combinatorics can forget symplectic sizes.

proof_stages.label: Hamiltonian moment-map framework
proof_stages.summary: Hamiltonian group actions are defined through moment maps, whose components are Morse-Bott functions tied to fixed sets and whose torus-action images satisfy the Atiyah-Guillemin-Sternberg convexity theorem.

proof_stages.label: Toric classification benchmark
proof_stages.summary: For effective torus actions the rank is at most half the manifold dimension, and in the maximal toric case Delzant’s theorem classifies manifolds up to equivariant symplectomorphism by simple rational smooth polytopes, with an explicit symplectic-reduction construction.

proof_stages.label: Toric equivariant cohomology and reconstruction limits
proof_stages.summary: The Danilov-Jurkiewicz face-ring presentation, its symplectic interpretation via Kirwan and Tolman-Weitsman, and Masuda’s reconstruction result show that equivariant cohomology with its algebra structure can recover the fan/diffeotype but not symplectic scale.

proof_stages.label: Complexity-one graph classification data
proof_stages.summary: The talk moves from toric actions to complexity-one actions, especially Hamiltonian circle actions on four-manifolds, where Karshon-style decorated graphs encode isolated and surface fixed sets, isotropy weights, moment labels, genus, and symplectic areas.

proof_stages.label: Dull-graph recovery from equivariant cohomology
proof_stages.summary: Goldin-Holm-Kessler methods give subring and generators-and-relations descriptions for complexity-one equivariant cohomology, and the resulting theorem says that, after forgetting moment/symplectic-size labels, the dull graph is equivalent to the equivariant cohomology algebra together with its odd-genus data.

proof_stages.label: Sharpness against symplectic or holomorphic classification
proof_stages.summary: Concrete toric-surface-derived circle actions with the same dull graph demonstrate that the cohomology/dull-graph data need not determine symplectic volume, Chern class, or complex-variety isomorphism.

proof_stages.label: Equivariant diffeotype theorem and orientation refinements
proof_stages.summary: The joint theorem with Kessler and Tolman proves that every dull-graph isomorphism is induced by an equivariant diffeomorphism, with a stronger orientation-sensitive four-way equivalence among dull graphs, equivariant cohomology algebras/modules, and equivariant diffeomorphism classes.

examples.name: Scaled polytopes with the same fan
examples.note: A larger turquoise polytope and smaller green polytope have the same inward-normal fan, making concrete why fan/combinatorial data forget symplectic size.
examples.engagement_reason: she explains the fan construction from normals and uses the larger-versus-smaller same-shape polytopes to reason that scaling preserves the fan but changes symplectic size

examples.name: McDuff and Tolman-Zhang non-Hamiltonian circle actions
examples.note: Higher-dimensional symplectic circle actions with fixed points but no real-valued moment map illustrate the limits of the Kähler/four-dimensional Hamiltonian fixed-point criterion.

examples.name: Rectangle versus trapezoid toric surfaces
examples.note: The rectangle and trapezoid have the same face ring but correspond to different toric surfaces, showing why the algebra-over-H_T(pt) structure, not just the abstract quotient ring, is needed.
examples.engagement_reason: she computes the face-ring relations for the rectangle and trapezoid and compares the resulting toric surfaces

passes.reason: the rectangle and trapezoid are ordinary Delzant quadrilaterals used to show that the face ring alone need not determine the toric surface diffeotype

examples.name: Same-dull-graph vertical-circle toric blowup pair
examples.note: Two toric four-manifolds restricted to a vertical circle action have the same dull graph and equivariant cohomology but different symplectic volumes and complex-variety structures, motivating the sharp diffeotype theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: she analyzes the vertical-circle restriction, identifies fixed components and dull-graph data, compares volumes and Chern classes, and explains the blowup-chain flip leading to an equivariant diffeomorphism

passes.reason: the two ordinary toric blowup manifolds with the same dull graph are used to show that equivariant cohomology/dull-graph data does not determine symplectic or complex structure
